title: The label almost everyone forgets
checkLabels.t2: The label knows which field is its own
checkLabels.t3: The field has a name to answer to
why.no-label: The fields have no labels. A paragraph above a field is not a label — it is just text that happens to sit on top. There is a tag for it: <label>.
why.not-tied: There are labels, but they do not know which field they belong to. for is missing. And without that connection a label is useless to exactly the person it was put there for.
why.no-id: The label points at a field, and the field has no id to answer to. The `for` on the label and the `id` on the field must match.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: The form works. There are captions above the fields — a person can see what to type. It looks fine.
blocks.1.text: But those captions are paragraphs. They sit above the field by coincidence, because you typed them first. To the browser there is no connection between them at all.
blocks.4.text: <label> is a field's label. The for attribute points at the field's id. The two must match — that is where the connection comes from.
blocks.5.text: Notice: the field now has TWO names. id is for the link to the label. name is for the data being sent. Different things, often the same word.
blocks.7.text: Run it and click on the LABEL — not the field. The cursor jumps into the field.
blocks.8.text: Trivial? Not on a phone. The field is small, the finger is large. A tied label doubles the area a person can hit.
blocks.9.text: And with a checkbox or radio button it matters even more — there the box is tiny.
blocks.10.text: And for the one who cannot see
blocks.11.text: A blind person reaches the field. Their computer reads it out: "text field".
blocks.12.text: Which field? For what? They do not know. They heard the paragraph above ten seconds ago and have no way of knowing it was related.
blocks.13.text: A form without labels is a form only a sighted person can fill in.
blocks.14.text: And a placeholder is not a label
blocks.15.text: Here is the mistake you will see on half the sites on the web.
blocks.16.text: A placeholder is the grey text INSIDE the field. It looks like a label and many people use it as one.
blocks.17.text: Except it vanishes the moment someone starts typing. If they pause halfway to think — they no longer know what the field wanted.
blocks.18.text: Many screen readers do not read it at all. Its contrast is poor. And it is grey, which means older people cannot see it well.
blocks.19.text: A placeholder is an EXAMPLE, not a label. "ivan@abv.bg" is a good placeholder. "Email" is not — that is <label>'s job.
blocks.20.text: Your turn. Give every field a real label.
blocks.21.text: Then click the label and watch the cursor jump. That small pleasure is the sign you did it right.
blocks.22.text: One tag. One attribute. And your form suddenly works for everyone.
